item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description really needs to explain what the anomaly would actually *do*. I mean, it would kill people, right? Why doesn't it say that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I still, of course, have no idea what the hell the anomaly is (and understand that is somewhat the point), the interactions between Postmodern Alchemy and the MTF are absolutely delightful and more than enough to secure an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for people who know what the Foundation does and that it's the best emergency response for their situation. Also for an atom massing 5 kg, holy shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might wanna put a footnote or similar about that in there, I came to the comments to see if there was an explanation as to what that was. Otherwise I enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Note to self: check the comments on my skips more often.)

-2 is basically a ball of quark soup. The cliff notes version is that if you squash enough matter and energy together, the strong force takes over and everything just collapses into a wad of quarks. The problem with -2…ever seen a video of prince rupert's drop? Stable as long as the surface tension is holding everything together, break the tension and it explodes. Directly switching off the field would be like that but with more nuclear fission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is -2, besides radioactive and dangerous outside of -1?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Those evolved microchips are actually the inspiration for the disconnected parts bit. ^_^
It's one of my favorite bits of weird science because they show how we've only begun to scratch the surface of what can be built.

One of my favorites is an experiment from the 1990s that evolved a simple voice recognition chip on a reprogrammable gate array with 100 gates. (For comparison, it would be difficult to program a basic digital clock on this thing.) The final generation only needed 37 of them, and looks like something designed by an alien. It bootlegged a simple grid of digital switches into a half-analog series of feedback loops communicating through induced current. After some poking and prodding we get how and why it works, but it's still like seeing a lawnmower taken apart and reassembled as an RC helicopter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I thought this was just going to be another version of 536 but with only one setting, but this one actually stands on its own fairly well. That it's possibly not anomalous but just poorly understood is an intriguing concept. The footnote mentioning extraneous parts that cause the system to fail if powered also reminds me of a real-life AI-designed microchip that had parts that weren't powered but caused the circuit to fail if not included, which provides a nice bit of subtext without being foreboding. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As with the previous staff, these individuals expressed great enthusiasm for the prospect of containment, one attempting to embrace several members of MTF Gamma-103 before being dissuaded.

The mental image of a dishevelled-looking wizard trying to cuddle a non-plussed task force member was hilarious enough, and in conjunction with the rest of the fun hard sci-fi stuff – abusing the strong force to create gold is certainly one of the more creative plans I've heard – secured my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Particle SCP-2871-2 is visually detected by P.A. staff."

That's an excellent oh shit moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love a good hard sci-fi SCP, especially ones with annotations that hold the hands of dopes like myself :P

However, my favorite part of this article and the thing that makes it truly memorable is Postmodern Alchemy. I'm a big fan of the idea of a paratech startup fucking something up and then nerding out when the "SPC" show up. Honestly, the entire recovery log was great.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
exotic matter that would probably decompose explosively in normal physical conditions

I thought about including a detailed explanation, but the resulting wall of technobabble didn't add much value and messed with the pacing of the story. In universe, there's some team of Foundation researchers generating a series of papers based on data gathered from the 2871-2 particle, but for the article's purposes the above is all that really needs to be understood.

If you're up for a crash course in quantum chromodynamics, though, check out Nuclear Matter and QCD matter for the closest approximation to what's happening there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, but I think you should give a direct detailing of SCP-2871-2 as it is mentioned in the recovery timeline. I can infer that it's some form of exotic matter that would probably decompose explosively in normal physical conditions, but if it's given a designation direct information about it is probably important!

Else simply state that the operation of SCP-2871 caused the formation of dangerous exotic matter?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's always a joy to see hard sci-fi skips get posted, and this one is especially entertaining to read. Also, good job at describing the science in an easy to understand manner.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hahaha, you actually did it! I love it. I love the base anomaly and device and "theories", I love the progressing action, and I love how blasted happy the employees were to see the Foundation. Also your hint above.

Also, the event log reminded me a lot of an account of the SL-1 reactor accident I've had to read for three separate classes, so points for verisimilitude.

+1, of course

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The company's name is a hint at what they intended to use this for, actually. ^_^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can imagine the trepidation with which the Postmodern Alchemy staff greeted a possible visit from a GOC MTF. Their approach to containment always makes me picture a plow and a bag of rock salt.

This is definitely on the hard science end of things. I liked it, although it made me wonder what the PA staff were aiming at that they hit this by mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can only say that if someone can build something that actually does this, there is something very, very wrong with the laws of physics.

"We were so caught up with the question of ""could we"" we din stop to ask ourselves ""should we"""

Yes. The answer is always "Yes". X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, this is super entertaining! I'm glad you found a nice balance to make it work; it just as easily couldn't have worked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well….I guess I get what your trying to do but the question is was this a "SCP" just to prove to someone that you can write weird stuff using hard science? Cuz personally this doesn't really feel scp'ish. More like science experiment that went wrong. Not scary. Or out of the ordinary. Or thought provoking. Large hadron collider goes beserk! That's it.

Not bashing you cuz I've read your work before and it's good, but I don't feel this should be here. 😛

"We were so caught up with the question of ""could we"" we din stop to ask ourselves ""should we"""

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've had a draft floating around the forum for quite a while now, so that might be where you saw it; first time posting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was this posted before (but somewhat different) or did I just read a draft of it like 8 months ago?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
!!SCIENCE!!

I'd like to thank Petrograd for inspiring this idea in the "SCP ideas that would never ever work" thread:

Any kind of SCP written entirely in hard-science language with no practical examples or implications

I ended up fudging a little in the name of story, but I think it captures the spirit of the prompt. X3

Also have to thank WrongJohnSilver and DrBleep for feedback.
